us-politics.headline: Iran Conflict Escalates as Trump Demands Record Defense Budget
us-politics.body:
Iran struck Gulf oil refineries this week as the U.S. military continued attacks on Iranian targets. Trump warned further strikes could hit bridges and power plants, while also claiming the military has not yet deployed its full force against Iran. The confrontation is driving a record budget request: $1.5 trillion for the Pentagon, the largest defense spending proposal in U.S. history.

The budget pairs that defense surge with deep cuts to domestic programs, drawing sharp criticism from Democrats and fiscal hawks alike. Supporters argue the spending reflects genuine security threats, pointing to Iran's regional aggression and Russian pressure on NATO's eastern flank. Critics say the numbers are politically driven and fiscally reckless.

sources.title: Trump requests record-breaking budget of $1.5 trillion for Pentagon
Trump calls for a major increase in defense spending alongside cuts in domestic spending
Live updates: White House budget expected, with up to $1.5T for defense
Trump: US military ‘hasn’t even started destroying what’s left in Iran’
Iran hits Gulf refineries as Trump warns U.S. will attack Iranian bridges, power plants

us-politics.headline: Bondi Firing Fuels Epstein File Controversy at DOJ
us-politics.body:
Attorney General Pam Bondi was removed from her post roughly 12 days before a scheduled Epstein-related deposition, a timing that Democrats including Rep. Seth Moulton and Rep. Ro Khanna say is no coincidence. Trump's deputy chief of staff Todd Blanche pushed back, saying he never heard Trump link Bondi's exit to the Epstein files and that those files should play no role in future DOJ activity.

The dismissal has triggered a broader shake-up at the Justice Department, with five major questions now hanging over its leadership and direction. Democrats are demanding answers. The White House says the move was routine. The gap between those two positions is wide.

sources.title: Moulton: Trump ‘got rid’ of Bondi 12 days before Epstein deposition
Blanche: Epstein files ‘should not be a part of anything going forward’ at DOJ
Blanche: I never heard Trump say Bondi exit ‘had anything to do with the Epstein files’
Whole Hog Politics: Bondi beached
Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., on the future of the Epstein inquiry with Bondi's ousting
Five questions hanging over the DOJ shake-up

us-politics.headline: Vance Takes on Voter Fraud Role, Targets Blue States
us-politics.body:
Trump announced that Vice President JD Vance will serve as a so-called fraud czar, with a focus on Democratic-leaning states. The move is framed by the White House as an election integrity push, though critics see it as a politically targeted effort to pressure opposition strongholds ahead of future elections.

The announcement lands alongside a Fox News report showing a House Democrat receiving unexpected constituent support for voter ID laws at a town hall - a result that cuts against the standard partisan framing on the issue. Vance is also scheduled to meet Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán in Budapest days before Hungary's parliamentary elections, adding an international dimension to his already high-profile week.

sources.title: Trump: Vance will primarily focus on blue states as ‘fraud czar’
WATCH: House Democrat gets unexpected response when he asks constituents about voter ID requirements
VP Vance to meet with Viktor Orbán in Hungary days ahead of foreign nation's elections

us-politics.headline: Strong Jobs Numbers Arrive Amid Broader Economic Uncertainty
us-politics.body:
The U.S. economy added 178,000 jobs in March, beating forecasts by a significant margin. The figure gives the White House a positive data point to highlight as it pushes its budget and defends its economic agenda against Democratic attacks.

The strong headline number sits alongside ongoing debates about federal spending, taxpayer-funded lobbying, and the long-term impact of AI on employment. Analysts warn that robust job creation today may mask structural shifts driven by automation - shifts that AI developers themselves say are accelerating faster than policymakers acknowledge.

sources.title: Taxpayers shouldn’t be forced to fund lobbyists
AI architects are sounding the alarm about jobs vanishing — here’s why no one listens
US adds 178K jobs in March, far more than expected

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites as Industry Warns Stocks Won't Last
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel reserves could run dry within a month, industry groups warned this week, raising alarm about the country's energy security. The warning comes as an Iran-linked conflict continues to disrupt supply chains across Asia, with ordinary workers and businesses already feeling the squeeze at the pump.

Energy Minister Chris Bowen has faced pressure to act, but critics argue the government has been slow to build strategic reserves. The crisis sits awkwardly alongside Labor's net zero commitments - a tension the Spectator has highlighted, pointing to the contradiction of climate-pledging nations scrambling for oil supply. Westpac CEO Andrew Miller added to the economic unease, warning publicly that a recession is a genuine possibility.

sources.title: Fuel heading to Australia won't last a month, industry says
A day in the life of Asia’s fuel crisis
Net Zero nations trapped in mad race for oil
'There's a chance of a recession,' warns Westpac boss

au-politics.headline: Trump Fires Senior Officials in Sweeping Administration Purge
au-politics.body:
Donald Trump has moved to dismiss a string of senior officials, including Attorney-General Pam Bondi, in what analysts describe as one of his most aggressive reshuffles since returning to office. Several of those removed were women who had been among his most vocal public defenders.

The firings have drawn sharp commentary across the political spectrum. Some see them as Trump consolidating loyalty ahead of key policy battles, while others argue they reflect an administration prone to instability. The ABC's analysis notes the particular irony of Bondi's removal given her staunch support for Trump during his legal battles.

sources.title: Donald Trump is going on a firing spree
Trump's fiercest female defenders are now on the outer

au-politics.headline: Myanmar Junta Chief Seizes Presidency After Sham Vote
au-politics.body:
Myanmar's military leader Min Aung Hlaing has been formally elected president, cementing his grip on power following the 2021 coup. The vote, conducted under junta control, has been widely dismissed by international observers as lacking any democratic legitimacy.

The move deepens concerns about the humanitarian situation inside Myanmar, where civil conflict has displaced millions. Australia and its regional partners face renewed pressure to respond, though concrete action from ASEAN nations has so far been limited.

sources.title: Myanmar junta chief Min Aung Hlaing elected president

au-politics.headline: Easter Long Weekend Marred by Fatal Road Crashes
au-politics.body:
At least four people died in separate road crashes across Australia over the Good Friday long weekend. Three fatalities were recorded in Western Australia alone, including a 14-year-old dirt bike rider. A motorcyclist also died in a crash on the Monaro Highway in southern New South Wales.

Authorities have urged motorists to slow down and take breaks, with police maintaining a heightened presence on major routes. The deaths come despite ongoing road safety campaigns ahead of one of the busiest travel periods of the year.

sources.title: Highway closed after motorcyclist dies in crash in southern NSW
Dirt bike rider, 14, among three dead in separate WA Easter long weekend crashes

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Legal and Civilian Toll Mounts
world.body:
American and Israeli strikes on Iran have hit civilian infrastructure including a sports hall in Lamerd, a bridge near Karaj, and medical supply chains. Experts dispute the US account of the Lamerd strike, with independent analysts saying the site had no military function. Over 100 US legal scholars have condemned the campaign as potential war crimes under international law.

Iranians near targeted sites describe growing fear about where strikes land next. Kuwait's desalination plant and oil refinery were also hit by missile and drone attacks, widening the conflict's regional footprint. The Trump administration has offered shifting timelines for ending the war, with no clear exit strategy publicly articulated.

sources.title: Experts dispute US account of deadly Iran sports hall strike in Lamerd
How the US and Israel are waging war on Iran’s medicines, vaccines
International law experts allege violations in Iran war
When will the Iran war end? Tracing the Trump administration's timelines
Over 100 US legal experts condemn strikes on Iran as possible ‘war crimes’
'This has got me worried': Iranians fear what comes next after US strike on Karaj bridge
Kuwait desalination plant, oil refinery hit by missile and drone strikes
Iran condemns US-Israeli ‘moral collapse’ after attacks on civilian sites

world.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Hegseth Pushes Out Army's Top General
world.body:
President Trump removed Attorney General Pam Bondi and replaced her with Todd Blanche, his former personal defense lawyer. The dismissal follows a pattern of loyalty-driven personnel changes across the administration. No formal explanation was given.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth separately asked the Army's top general to step down, with General Christopher LaNeve set to take the role. The back-to-back removals of senior officials in both the Justice Department and military leadership signal continued pressure on institutional figures seen as insufficiently aligned with the White House.

sources.title: Pete Hegseth asks US Army's top general to step down
Who is Christopher LaNeve, set to lead the US Army?
Trump removes US Attorney General Pam Bondi
Why did Trump fire Pam Bondi from Justice Department, who is Todd Blanche?

world.headline: Burkina Faso Rejects Democracy as Gaza and Lebanon Rebuild Under Fire
world.body:
Burkina Faso's military leader Ibrahim Traore told supporters to abandon the idea of democracy, cementing the junta's grip since its 2022 coup. The statement marks one of the bluntest rejections of electoral governance by any sitting head of state in recent memory.

Elsewhere, Palestinians in Gaza are rebuilding homes from rubble, mud, and salvaged materials after sustained bombardment. In southern Lebanon, Israeli strikes continue to target healthcare infrastructure, compounding a humanitarian crisis that predates the current escalation. France's courts overturned a government ban on a Muslim gathering, offering a rare legal check on restrictions targeting religious assembly.

sources.title: France's Muslim gathering ban overturned by courts
Burkina Faso military leader Traore says ‘forget democracy’
Rubble, mud and hair: How to rebuild a home in Gaza
Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says
How Israel is destroying healthcare infrastructure in southern Lebanon

world.headline: Artemis II Breaks Orbit; Italy Sacks Gattuso After World Cup Failure
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew fired the spacecraft's engines and left Earth's orbit, advancing toward the far side of the Moon in the first crewed lunar mission since Apollo. The milestone marks a significant step in the US-led return to deep space exploration.

On the ground, Italy sacked coach Gennaro Gattuso after the national team failed to qualify for the 2026 World Cup - the second consecutive tournament Italy has missed. Cuba announced pardons for over 2,000 prisoners under mounting US diplomatic pressure, and the Panama Papers marked their tenth anniversary with analysts noting limited systemic accountability for the offshore finance networks they exposed.

sources.title: Gattuso out as Italy’s coach after failure to qualify for 2026 World Cup
Artemis II leaves Earth's orbit on track for far side of the Moon
Artemis II mission fires up engines in next step of moon mission
Ten years since Panama Papers: What did they reveal, did anything change?
Cuba to pardon more than 2,000 prisoners amid US pressure
Cuba to release more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts

ai.headline: OpenAI Buys Talk Show, Raising Conflict-of-Interest Alarms
ai.body:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a founder-focused tech talk show with a loyal startup-world audience. The deal is being framed internally as a media strategy play, but critics are blunt: the company just bought itself a friendly microphone. Wired called it purchasing positive news coverage outright.

The acquisition fits a pattern of OpenAI expanding well beyond its core AI research mission - into consumer hardware, lobbying, and now media. TBPN's hosts have been prominent cheerleaders for the AI industry, which makes the editorial independence question hard to ignore. Whether the show maintains any critical distance from its new owner is the only question that matters.

sources.title: OpenAI takes on another "side quest," buys tech-focused talk show TBPN
OpenAI Acquires Tech Talk Show ‘TBPN’—and Buys Itself Some Positive News
OpenAI acquires TBPN, the buzzy founder-led business talk show

ai.headline: AI Chatbots Are Now Prescribing Psychiatric Medication
ai.body:
AI chatbots are moving into clinical territory that regulators have barely begun to map. The Verge reports that some services are now prescribing and refilling psychiatric drugs, a development that blurs the line between mental health support tool and licensed medical provider.

The risks are significant. Psychiatric medications require careful dosing, monitoring for side effects, and ongoing clinical judgment. Handing that process to a chatbot - even a capable one - introduces failure modes that could seriously harm vulnerable users. Separately, a lawsuit against Perplexity alleges its "incognito mode" offers no real privacy protection, a reminder that AI products routinely oversell safety features to users who trust them.

sources.title: Chatbots are now prescribing psychiatric drugs
Perplexity's "Incognito Mode" is a "sham," lawsuit says

ai.headline: Rowhammer Attack Hands Attackers Full Control Over Nvidia GPU Machines
ai.body:
Researchers have demonstrated a new class of Rowhammer attacks that can give an attacker complete control of systems running Nvidia GPUs. Rowhammer exploits physical memory vulnerabilities by repeatedly accessing memory rows to flip bits in adjacent rows - a technique once considered theoretical that has grown steadily more practical.

The Nvidia angle is particularly sharp given how central GPU clusters are to AI training infrastructure. A successful attack on a shared cloud GPU instance could compromise not just one tenant but potentially the entire host. The disclosure adds hardware-level memory attacks to a threat landscape that AI infrastructure operators are only beginning to take seriously.

sources.title: New Rowhammer attacks give complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs

ai.headline: EU Caves to US Pressure on Digital Rules, Critics Warn
ai.body:
European critics are accusing the EU of quietly softening its digital regulations under pressure from the United States, a shift that could blunt the enforcement teeth of laws like the Digital Markets Act and the AI Act. Politico reports that the concessions are being made without public debate, alarming regulators and civil society groups who see the rules as a rare check on American tech giants.

The timing matters. US tech companies have spent years lobbying against EU digital rules, and the current political climate in Washington has given them unusual leverage. If Brussels backs down now, it hands the industry a template for dismantling tech regulation through diplomatic pressure rather than legal challenge.

sources.title: Critics say EU risks ceding control of its tech laws under U.S. pressure

btc.headline: Bitcoin Drifts Into Holiday Weekend With Bears Circling
btc.body:
Bitcoin traded flat heading into the Good Friday long weekend, with ETF inflows and CME futures markets going dark until Monday. Futures positioning has tilted bearish, volatility has cooled, and on-chain data shows the share of Bitcoin supply held in profit approaching levels analysts associate with true bear markets. The timing is uncomfortable. With institutional flow channels offline, thin liquidity leaves Bitcoin exposed to sharp moves in either direction.

Cathie Wood pushed back on the gloom, arguing Bitcoin is done with the 85% drawdowns of earlier cycles and setting a new floor target of $34,000. Meanwhile, solo miner luck provided a rare bright spot - one independent operator cracked a block and walked away with a $210,000 reward.

sources.title: Crypto snoozes into Good Friday as oil and macro stir: Crypto Daybook Americas
Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish
Bitcoin supply in profit heads to ‘true bear market’ levels
Bitcoin heads into holiday weekend exposed as ETF and CME flows go offline
Bitcoin 'done' with 85% crashes, says Cathie Wood amid new $34K target
Solo Bitcoin miner bags $210K Bitcoin block reward

btc.headline: Stablecoins Flip ACH Volume as Circle Targets Wrapped Bitcoin
btc.body:
Stablecoins processed more transaction volume than the US automated clearing house network in February, a milestone that underscores how crypto payment rails are moving from novelty to infrastructure. The IMF acknowledged the efficiency gains from tokenization this week but flagged new systemic risks that regulators have yet to fully address.

Circle moved to deepen its institutional footprint by announcing cirBTC, a wrapped Bitcoin token that puts it in direct competition with BitGo's WBTC and Coinbase's cbBTC. The launch signals Circle is not content to own stablecoins alone. Separately, the Ethereum Foundation completed staking 70,000 ETH worth roughly $93 million, reinforcing its commitment to the network's proof-of-stake security.

sources.title: Stablecoins flip automated clearing house volume in February
Tokenization makes finance more efficient but introduces risks: IMF
Circle to launch cirBTC wrapped Bitcoin, challenging BitGo and Coinbase
Ethereum Foundation stakes another $93 million ether, reaching its 70,000 ETH target

btc.headline: DeFi Hacks Hit $169M in Q1 as Drift Chases $280M Exploiter
btc.body:
Hackers drained $169 million from 34 DeFi protocols in the first quarter of 2026, according to DefiLlama data - a decline from prior periods but still a damaging pace. Drift Protocol took an unusual step in response to its own $280 million exploit, sending an on-chain message directly to wallets linked to the attacker in an attempt to open negotiations.

A Bank of Canada study added nuance to DeFi's risk picture, finding that Aave V3 avoided bad debt during volatile periods largely by shifting liquidation losses onto borrowers rather than the protocol itself. The finding raises questions about who actually bears the tail risk in decentralized lending.

sources.title: Crypto hackers steal $169M from 34 DeFi protocols in Q1: DefiLlama
Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit
Aave avoided bad debt by shifting risk to borrowers: Bank of Canada study

btc.headline: Hyperliquid Cracks Derivatives Top 10 as Quantum Threat Looms
btc.body:
Binance dominated crypto derivatives in Q1, but the quarter's standout story was Hyperliquid breaking into the top 10 exchanges by volume - a sign that decentralized perpetuals are gaining serious ground on centralized incumbents. Offchain Labs argued this week that Ethereum Layer 2 networks will need more responsive fee pricing to handle the transaction volumes required for mainstream scale.

On the security frontier, Naoris Protocol launched a quantum-resistant blockchain as concerns grow that advances in quantum computing could eventually threaten Bitcoin and Ethereum's cryptographic foundations. South Korean brokerage Korea Investment and Securities also made headlines, reportedly eyeing a stake in local exchange Coinone as traditional finance continues to push into crypto.

sources.title: Binance led Q1 crypto derivatives as Hyperliquid cracked top 10: CoinGlass
Ethereum L2s need responsive pricing to scale, says Offchain Labs
Naoris Protocol's quantum-resistant blockchain goes live as Bitcoin and Ethereum face 'Q-Day' threats
South Korean brokerage Korea Investment & Securities eyes Coinone stake: Report

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, buttery cod fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until caramelised and lacquered
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the fish sits, the deeper the umami crust when it hits the heat

exercise.name: The Deck of Cards Workout
exercise.equipment: One standard deck of playing cards, exercise mat
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight move to each suit, flip cards one by one, and perform reps matching the card value — randomness keeps your heart rate unpredictable and your brain engaged

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — philosophers have extended this to human cells, memories, and consciousness itself
learn.why: On a Friday when the week has quietly changed you, it's a surprisingly personal rabbit hole: neuroscience, Derek Parfit's work on personal identity, and the unsettling question of whether the you of ten years ago is genuinely the same person reading this now
